description: Generic SATA SSD health monitoring template. Uses the agent's PhysicalDisk.* metric family (which wraps smartctl --all -j) to collect identity, capacity, SMART pass/fail status, temperature, power-on time, power cycle count and the four most universal ATA SMART error counters by numeric ID (5, 197, 198, 199). Uses raw SMART values (PhysicalDisk.SmartAttrRaw) instead of the normalized 0..100 value, because most counters stay saturated at 100 until failure. Attribute counters are referenced by their numeric SMART ID rather than smartctl's vendor-specific name, so the same DCI works on Samsung, Kingston, Crucial, Intel, etc. without per-vendor renaming - any disk that exposes the standard ID will report. Instance discovery is via the PhysicalDisk.Devices agent list, filtered to devices reported by smartctl as type 'sat'/'ata' (so NVMe and pure SCSI are skipped). The agent must run with sufficient privileges to invoke smartctl on the target devices (root on Linux, Administrator on Windows). The template is not auto-bound - bind manually to nodes that have SATA SSDs you want to monitor.

templates.name: SATA SSD Health
templates.comments:
Generic SATA SSD monitoring via the NetXMS agent's PhysicalDisk.* metrics.

Instance discovery:
  - Source: PhysicalDisk.Devices agent list (smartctl --scan).
  - Filter: only devices whose smartctl device type is 'sat', 'ata' or 'atapi' (HDDs are also matched - the metrics here apply to both, just disable the wear-related thresholds if used on a spinning disk). NVMe drives are excluded because their SMART data lives in nvme_smart_health_information_log, not in ata_smart_attributes/table, and the SmartAttrRaw/Threshold/Worst metrics only read the ATA table.
  - Display name: device path followed by model in parentheses, e.g. '/dev/sdb (KINGSTON SNV425S264GB)'.

Metric coverage and thresholds:
  Identity (polled hourly - changes rarely):
    PhysicalDisk.Model         -> model name
    PhysicalDisk.Firmware      -> firmware version
    PhysicalDisk.SerialNumber  -> serial number
    PhysicalDisk.Capacity      -> user capacity in bytes
  Health:
    PhysicalDisk.SmartStatus   -> SMART overall pass/fail (every 5 min); 'true'/'false' is converted to 1/0 by transformation. Threshold fires when value != 1.
    PhysicalDisk.Temperature   -> warning >= 60 degC, critical >= 70 degC (consumer SSDs typically rated to 70 degC; tighten if your environment requires it).
  Wear / lifetime (polled hourly):
    PhysicalDisk.PowerOnTime   -> hours
    PhysicalDisk.PowerCycles   -> count
  Error counters - all use PhysicalDisk.SmartAttrRaw with the numeric ATA SMART attribute ID (not the vendor-specific name). Threshold fires on any non-zero raw count:
    ID 5   (Reallocated_Sector_Ct)    - sectors remapped due to media errors
    ID 197 (Current_Pending_Sector)   - sectors awaiting reallocation; non-zero is critical
    ID 198 (Offline_Uncorrectable)    - sectors that could not be read or remapped
    ID 199 (UDMA / CRC_Error_Count)   - SATA link CRC errors (cable/connector problem, not the drive itself)

Not included (intentionally - too vendor-specific to be portable):
  - Wear leveling / SSD life remaining (Samsung uses attr 177 'Wear_Leveling_Count', Intel uses 233 'Media_Wearout_Indicator', Kingston/JMicron uses 173 'Erase_Count' with a packed raw, etc.)
  - Total LBAs Written / Read (attribute 241/242 on most but not all)
  - Vendor-specific Bad_Block_Count style counters
  Add per-vendor sub-templates that extend this one if you need those - using numeric IDs on those too will make them robust against drivedb naming differences.

Notes:
  - The agent caches smartctl output for 60 seconds per device, so polling 12 metrics per disk results in one smartctl invocation per minute per disk.
  - DCIs that reference an ATA SMART attribute by ID will return UNSUPPORTED on disks where the firmware does not expose that attribute at all (e.g. Samsung does not expose 197/198, Kingston/JMicron does not expose 198/199). That is expected; nothing to alarm on.
  - PhysicalDisk.SmartAttrRaw and friends accept either a numeric ID (1..255) or a smartctl attribute name. This template uses numeric IDs throughout for portability.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.Model({instance})
dcis.comments: Model name reported by smartctl (model_name field in JSON output). Polled hourly - this value does not change at runtime.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.Firmware({instance})
dcis.comments: Firmware version (firmware_version field in smartctl JSON output). Polled hourly - changes only on firmware update.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SerialNumber({instance})
dcis.comments: Serial number (serial_number field in smartctl JSON output). Polled hourly.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.Capacity({instance})
dcis.comments: User capacity in bytes (user_capacity/bytes in smartctl JSON output). 64-bit unsigned to accommodate disks > 4 TiB. Polled hourly.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartStatus({instance})
dcis.comments: Overall SMART pass/fail flag from the drive firmware (smart_status/passed in smartctl JSON output). The agent returns the JSON boolean as the literal string 'true' or 'false', so the raw data type is STRING; the transformation script converts to 1 (passed) / 0 (failed) and stores as INT so a numeric threshold can be applied. Polled every 5 minutes. Threshold fires on any value other than 1 - this includes both an explicit firmware FAIL and any condition that prevents smartctl from returning a status (drive disappeared, lost SMART connection, etc.).

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.Temperature({instance})
dcis.comments: Drive temperature in degrees Celsius (temperature/current in smartctl JSON output). Most consumer SSDs are rated to 70 degC operating; thresholds set to warning >= 60, critical >= 70 to give some margin. Tighten if your application or environmental envelope requires it.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.PowerOnTime({instance})
dcis.comments: Power-on time in hours (power_on_time/hours in smartctl JSON output). Polled hourly.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.PowerCycles({instance})
dcis.comments: Number of power-on cycles (power_cycle_count in smartctl JSON output). Polled hourly.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttrRaw({instance},5)
dcis.comments:
Raw value of standard ATA SMART attribute ID 5 (typically named Reallocated_Sector_Ct). Number of sectors the drive has remapped from its spare pool because the original sector failed to read or write. On an SSD this represents flash blocks retired due to media errors. Healthy drives report 0; any non-zero value is worth investigating, and a steadily increasing count means the drive is heading for failure. Threshold fires on any non-zero value.

Looked up by numeric ID rather than name so it works regardless of how the drive's smartctl drivedb entry labels the attribute.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttrRaw({instance},197)
dcis.comments:
Raw value of standard ATA SMART attribute ID 197 (typically named Current_Pending_Sector). Number of sectors the drive has flagged as unstable and is waiting to remap on the next write attempt. A non-zero value means there are sectors that could not be read - data loss may have already occurred. Threshold fires on any non-zero value.

Looked up by numeric ID rather than name. Some SSD families (notably Samsung) do not expose attribute 197 at all - they track media errors via attribute 187 (Uncorrectable_Error_Cnt) instead - so this DCI will go inactive (UNSUPPORTED) on those disks. Add a per-vendor extension template if you need 187 coverage there.

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttrRaw({instance},198)
dcis.comments:
Raw value of standard ATA SMART attribute ID 198 (typically named Offline_Uncorrectable). Number of sectors found unreadable during an offline scan that could not be remapped. Indicates outright data loss; threshold fires on any non-zero value.

Looked up by numeric ID. Goes UNSUPPORTED on disks that do not expose this attribute - this is common (Kingston/JMicron, Samsung and others omit it entirely).

dcis.name: PhysicalDisk.SmartAttrRaw({instance},199)
dcis.comments:
Raw value of standard ATA SMART attribute ID 199 (variously named UDMA_CRC_Error_Count, CRC_Error_Count, etc. depending on vendor). Number of CRC errors detected on the SATA link itself. Indicates a cable, connector or backplane problem rather than a drive media problem - the drive itself may be perfectly healthy. Threshold fires on any non-zero count; if it stays flat after triggering you can safely acknowledge the alarm, if it keeps growing replace the cable.

Looked up by numeric ID rather than name - covers vendors that label this attribute differently from the Western Digital / Seagate canonical 'UDMA_CRC_Error_Count'.
